query: common complications after delivery for women with history of preeclampsia

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Eclamptic seizures can occur before, during, or after delivery, with a significant number occurring during the antepartum period. Postpartum eclampsia, particularly late-onset eclampsia, defined as seizures occurring more than 48 hours after delivery, can still pose a significant risk. A large portion of these seizures occurs in the first 7 to 10 days postpartum, often presenting with neurological symptoms, including headache. Postpartum headaches should be carefully evaluated, especially if accompanied by altered mental status, seizures, or visual disturbances, to rule out other severe conditions such as cerebral venous thrombosis or reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with preeclampsia, prevalent historical indicators often include the emergence of a new-onset headache resistant to conventional medications, distinct from pre-existing headache conditions or migraines. Visual disturbances may accompany this complaint. Patients may report discomfort in the right upper quadrant or epigastric region, coupled with feelings of nausea or vomiting. Shortness of breath and an escalation in swelling, exceeding baseline pregnancy-related symptoms, may also be articulated by affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive disorders affect as many as 10% of all pregnancies worldwide. [1] [7] These disorders account for a significant proportion of perinatal morbidity and mortality, nearly 10% of all maternal deaths in the United States. [1] Preeclampsia is one of the most serious pregnancy-specific medical conditions of increasing incidence. It remains a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality. [8] The risk of preeclampsia is increased in women with a previous history of preeclampsia (particularly if severe or before 32 weeks' gestation)and in those with antiphospholipid antibodies, pre-existing diabetes mellitus, multiple gestation, nulliparity, family history of preeclampsia (first-generation relative), elevated body mass index, maternal age greater than 40 years, and chronic hypertension or renal disease. [3] Women with a history of eclampsia are at increased risk of eclampsia (1% to 2%) and preeclampsia (22% to 35%) in subsequent pregnancies. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal history: Age, gravidity, parity, history of hypertension, diabetes, hypercoagulability, autoimmune disease, or cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common history findings in patients with preeclampsia are patient complaints of a new-onset headache not accountable by any other alternative diagnosis (ie, history of headaches or migraines) that is unresponsive to medication. This complaint may or may not be accompanied by additional complaints of visual disturbance. Patients may also endorse right upper quadrant or epigastric pain with associated nausea or vomiting. Shortness of breath and a perceived increase in swelling, both worsening from baseline pregnancy-related symptoms, may also be reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Epidemiology -- Eclampsia Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Eclampsia and other hypertensive disorders of pregnancy have many of the same risk factors: chronic hypertension, younger age, those with lower education levels, and first-time mothers. [10] According to the results from a recent eclampsia study, approximately one-third of eclampsia cases occurred in women younger than 20. [9] Other risk factors include: Personal history of pregnancy hypertensive disorder Family history of preeclampsia Prepregnancy chronic hypertension Comorbid conditions such as diabetes, renal disease, and autoimmune disorders Maternal obesity (body mass index >30 kg/m 2 ) History of preterm birth less than 32 weeks of gestation [11] Low socioeconomic status Insufficient prenatal care [11] Maternal age older than 35 Multifetal gestation Interval since previous birth of 10 or more years African American ethnicity In vitro fertilization [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The medical history or current health details of a patient experiencing an AFE may reveal factors such as advanced maternal age, multiple pregnancies, placenta-related issues (accreta, abruption, previa), preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, polyhydramnios, amniocentesis, use of amnioinfusion, amniotomy, cervical lacerations, or any surgery on the gravid uterus. [16] In the classic scenario, women in the later stages of labor suddenly develop acute shortness of breath accompanied by hypotension. Preceding other symptoms, there may be signs of agitation, anxiety, altered mental status, or a sense of impending doom. Seizures may ensue, leading to cardiac arrest, followed by massive hemorrhage associated with DIC, ultimately resulting in death—often within an hour of onset. Statistics indicate that 53% of females with AFE present at or just before delivery, while the remainder present, on average, 19 minutes after delivery. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Although preeclampsia typically presents with a hallmark history and physical signs and symptoms, several atypical presentations exist. This section will review the most common presentations of preeclampsia and those that should prompt further investigation and diagnostic testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical history: Preterm delivery, stillbirth, or a fetus affected by growth restriction, as well as any pregnancy complicated by preeclampsia, diabetes, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, or blood transfusion

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who present with any single feature or combination of these complaints should undergo a thorough physical exam. This begins with an evaluation of vital signs, specifically blood pressure. Patients with a SBP  ≥140 mmHg or a DBP ≥90 mmHg should raise suspicion for preeclampsia. In patients at or further than 20 weeks gestation, blood pressure readings on 2 measurements at least 4 hours apart should be evaluated with further diagnostic workup.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: While hypertension often accompanies eclampsia and is considered a distinguishing feature of the condition, it is not always present; some patients may develop eclampsia without severe blood pressure elevation. Conversely, not all patients with preeclampsia with severe features develop eclampsia. [10] [11] Moreover, proteinuria, a standard marker of preeclampsia, may also be absent in some cases of eclampsia. Therefore, the clinical characteristics of eclampsia are not dependable indicators. Additionally, the absence of severe preeclampsia features in some patients with eclampsia challenges the common belief that preeclampsia will always worsen over time, eventually progressing to eclampsia. [2] Clinicians should be careful to consider differential diagnoses, including posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, that present similarly to eclampsia and require careful management, including blood pressure control and long-term follow-up. [2] [11] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome ," for more information on this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Eclampsia
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery Considerations Delivery timing: Eclampsia is an indication for delivery; cesarean delivery is not necessarily required. The mode of delivery should be based on various clinical features, including gestational age, fetal presentation, and cervical favorability. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Bishop Score ," for more information on cervical favorability. Due to the increased risk for labor induction failure, in pregnancies under 30 weeks gestation with an unfavorable cervix, cesarean delivery is recommended. [2] [11] Maternal resuscitation : The maternal-fetal condition should be stabilized before proceeding with delivery. Following stabilization, fetal heart rate and uterine activity typically normalize. [2] [11] Antenatal steroids : A full course of corticosteroids should be administered in pregnancies less than 34 weeks gestation for fetal lung maturation; delivery should not be delayed to complete the steroid course if the maternal-fetal condition begins to deteriorate. [2] Postpartum monitoring : In the postpartum period, seizures most commonly occur in the first 48 hours following delivery, and the seizure risk remains elevated during the first postpartum week. Due to this and the increased risk of seizure in patients with uncontrolled hypertension, clinicians should continue monitoring patients frequently during the postpartum period. [1] Clinical features of pulmonary edema should be especially looked for in patients with renal dysfunction due to the increased risk of this complication. Adjust antihypertensive treatment accordingly to prevent further complications. [2] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Eclampsia(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: There may be a history of worsening headaches with vision changes such as blurred vision or “spots.” Hypertension and proteinuria may be present or absent. Impending eclampsia may have clonus. Eclampsia is a clinical diagnosis described by the occurrence of new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with preeclampsia; however, on occasion, it may be the first presentation of preeclampsia. Clinical findings may include posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (due to vasogenic edema predominantly localized in the posterior cerebral hemispheres), including headaches, confusion, visual symptoms, and seizure.

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Recent reevaluation of diagnostic blood pressure readings had expanded to include sustained severe hypertensive readings within minutes of repeat readings to allow for timely intervention with antihypertensive therapy. These blood pressure readings include SBP ≥160 mmHg or DBP ≥110 mmHg. Per ACOG, patients previously diagnosed with gestational hypertension presenting with these severe range blood pressures should be diagnosed with preeclampsia with severe features, regardless of the presence of other diagnostic criteria. When patients present with shortness of breath, auscultation and percussion of the lungs should be undertaken to examine for pulmonary disturbances. Palpation of the right upper quadrant and epigastric areas should be performed to evaluate for tenderness. An evaluation for edema should also be completed, specifically evaluating areas of dependent (gravity-related) edema, such as the lower extremities, or independent edema, such as in the face or hands. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium” encompasses a range of adverse conditions that can arise during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period, including the specific complication “Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” which is recognized as a distinct type of obstetric complication. Within this broader group, the subcategory “COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY” is positioned as a child of the overall complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that it focuses on adverse events that are primarily associated with the gestational phase itself. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of obstetric complications, linking general categories to more specific clinical presentations that are important for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in maternal health care.

These obstetric entities are also reflected in Newborn Screening Terminology, which incorporates maternal conditions such as pre‑eclampsia and mild hyperemesis gravidarum, underscoring the link between maternal health surveillance and neonatal assessment. Clinical databases are associated with all of the above concepts, providing repositories for documentation of normal and complicated deliveries, early signs of hypertensive disorders, infectious complications, multiple‑gestation outcomes, and postpartum events, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care, safety surveillance, and research within obstetric practice.

This integrated knowledge base supports precise coding, epidemiologic surveillance, risk assessment, diagnosis, management pathways, and targeted newborn‑screening strategies for clinicians caring for pregnant patients.

Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium belongs to the broader class “disease of the circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium,” and is further related to the inverse condition “hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” underscoring hypertension’s central role among pregnancy‑associated circulatory disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 559
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompass several serious obstetric conditions, most notably Hemorrhage during pregnancy—including abruptio placenta and placenta previa—and Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a specific form of Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth and puerperium, representing a circulatory‑system disease that arises or worsens during gestation and the postpartum period. This hypertensive disorder is further classified under the broader label “hypertension complicating pregnancy; childbirth and the puerperium.” Recognizing that hemorrhagic events such as abruptio placenta and placenta previa, together with hypertensive disorders of the circulatory system, are key complications of pregnancy is essential for effective obstetric assessment, risk stratification, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2066
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium encompass a wide range of adverse conditions that may arise during gestation, labor, or the postpartum period. Hypertension and/or vomiting that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are recognized as specific subtypes within this broader category. In other words, hypertension and vomiting occurring in the context of pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium are classified under the umbrella of complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, highlighting their hierarchical relationship and aiding clinicians in identifying and managing these particular obstetric adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader clinical category of Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it represents a key set of conditions that guide obstetric management. Within this overarching group, specific complications such as Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium are recognized as distinct subtypes, meaning that episodes of hypertension, vomiting, or their combination occurring during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period are considered particular manifestations of the general complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Understanding this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians identify and address hypertension and vomiting as important obstetric complications when planning and delivering care for pregnant and postpartum patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base organizes obstetric complications within a hierarchical framework that links specific maternal conditions, gestational findings, and fetal outcomes to broader categories used for diagnosis, risk assessment, and management. Complications mainly related to pregnancy are a subset of the larger groups “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium,” “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium,” “Early or threatened labor,” “Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa,” and “Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium.” Within these groups, distinct entities are recognized, such as Stenosis of cervix in pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth and puerperium, and Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium (a circulatory‑system disease occurring during the maternal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency, postpartum sepsis, retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage, prolonged pregnancy, other previous delivery, unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and malignant tumor of the placenta are all classified as members of the “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) (SMQ).” This SMQ category groups together a range of obstetric conditions that represent significant complications or risk factors during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period, emphasizing their importance for clinical assessment, monitoring, and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, serving as valuable resources for understanding and managing these entities. They are linked to مقدمات تسمم الحمل (early signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature placental separation), providing data that aid in early detection, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making for these serious pregnancy complications. In addition, databases are associated with تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (contraction of the pelvic inlet), offering information on uterine and pelvic dynamics that can influence labor progression and delivery planning. Finally, they are connected to gestation twin (twin pregnancy), supplying comprehensive records that support the monitoring of multiple fetuses, assessment of maternal‑fetal health, and coordination of specialized care. By integrating data across these conditions, medical databases enhance clinicians’ ability to deliver informed, evidence‑based obstetric care.
